Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Maruyama Sakura]][[en:draft:Maruyama Sakura]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Maruyama Sakura|Maruyama Sakura]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Cite sources clearly|date=September 2015}} {{Politician | Native name = まるやま さくら | Image = Maruyama_Sakura.jpg | Image size = 200px | Country abbreviation = {{JPN}} | Birthdate = [[October 27]], [[1840]] | Birthplace = {{JPN}} Edo, Shiba Mita (present-day [[Minato, Tokyo|Minato, Tokyo]]) | Deathdate = {{Death date and age|1840|10|27|1899|8|19}} | Previous position = [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Ministry of Foreign Affairs]] [[Daisuke (official)|Daijō]] | Political party = [[Rikken Teiseitō]] | Titles and honors = [[Jūshi-i]]
[[Commemorative Medals#Promulgation of the Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Constitution Promulgation Medal]] | Office = [[House of Peers (Japan)|Member of the House of Peers]] | Start date = [[September 29]], [[1890]] | End date = [[August 19]], 1899 | Flag = JPN | Office2 = [[Gikanin|Gikan]] | Start date2 = [[June 12]], [[1890]] | End date2 = [[October 20]], 1890 | Flag2 = JPN }} '''Maruyama Sakura''' (丸山 作楽, {{ill|1840|ja|天保}} 11年 {{ill|October 3 (old calendar)|ja|10月3日 (旧暦)}} – [[August 19]], [[1899]]) was a [[Meiji period]] [[diplomat]], [[businessman]], and [[politician]]. He was also known by the names Masatora, Masamichi, and Sakura. His titles included Shinsūsho, Sokō, and Iwanoya. His wife's name was Umekichi (Okutsuki). == Career == Maruyama was born in Edo’s Shiba Mita district as the eldest son of Maruyama Masanao, a samurai of the {{ill|Shimabara Domain|ja|島原藩}}. During the late Edo period, he studied {{ill|Kokugaku|ja|国学}} of the {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}} school and became active in the royalist movement. In [[1869]] (Meiji 2), he became [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Daisuke]] and was dispatched to {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}} to engage in negotiations with Russia. Returning in [[1870]] (Meiji 3), he advocated an aggressive policy against Russian expansion, but his proposals were not adopted. He also supported {{ill|Sada Hakaru|ja|佐田白茅}}’s {{ill|Seikanron|ja|征韓論}} and urged its implementation, but this too was rejected. In November of the same year, he was reassigned to {{ill|Ken-Daisuke (Ministry of Foreign Affairs)|ja|外務権大丞}} due to the failure of the Karafuto negotiations. Maruyama, along with {{ill|Hatake Kiyotada|ja|畑経世}}, a samurai of the {{ill|Sasayama Domain|ja|篠山藩}}, attempted to carry out the rejected Seikanron through rebellion. However, in [[1871]] (Meiji 4), their plans were uncovered, and Maruyama was arrested on [[May 10]] while on duty at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. On [[May 29]], he was dismissed from his position as Ken-Daisuke. The incident, considered politically minor, led to his sentencing to life imprisonment. He was released in [[1880]] (Meiji 13) through amnesty. After his release, Maruyama opposed the {{ill|Freedom and People's Rights Movement|ja|自由民権運動}} by establishing the {{ill|Chūaisha|ja|忠愛社}} and publishing the newspaper {{ill|Meiji Nippo|ja|明治日報}}. In [[1882]] (Meiji 15), he co-founded the {{ill|Rikken Teiseitō|ja|立憲帝政党}} with {{ill|Fukuchi Gen'ichirō|ja|福地源一郎}} and worked as a conservative politician. In [[1890]] (Meiji 23), he was appointed {{ill|Gikanin|ja|元老院議官}} on [[June 12]] and became {{ill|Non-working official|ja|非職}} when the {{ill|Gikan|ja|元老院}} was abolished on [[October 20]]. He was subsequently named {{ill|Imperial appointee to the House of Peers|ja|貴族院勅選議員}} on [[September 29]], serving until his death on [[August 19]], [[1899]], due to valvular heart disease. Maruyama was also known as a {{ill|Manyōshū|ja|万葉集}}-style poet. == Honors == ; Rank * [[June 19]], [[1890]] (Meiji 23): [[Jūshi-i]]『官報』第2094号「叙任及辞令」1890年6月24日。 ; Medals * [[November 29]], [[1889]] (Meiji 22): {{ill|Constitution Promulgation Medal|ja|記念章#賞勲局所管の記念章}}『官報』第1935号「叙任及辞令」1889年12月9日。 == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Sources == * Shūgiin and Sangiin Editors, *{{ill|100-Year History of the Japanese Diet|ja|議会制度百年史}} - {{ill|House of Peers and Sangiin Member Directory|ja|貴族院・参議院議員名鑑}}*, 1990. * Maseo Watanabe and Junko Hirose, Editors, *{{ill|Records of Imperial Appointments|ja|国立公文書館所蔵 勅奏任官履歴原書}} Vol. 1*, Kashiwa Shobō, 1995. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Shimabara Samurai Residence Street|ja|島原武家屋敷街}} - Maruyama opened the private school {{ill|Shinsūsho|ja|神習処}} here in 1861. == External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/kensei/maruyamasakura National Diet Library Constitutional Archive Maruyama Sakura Documents (Microfilm: Private Collection)] {{S-start}} {{S-off}} {{Succession box | title = {{Flagicon|JPN}} {{ill|Lower Chamber Deputy Minister|ja|集議院下局次官}} | years = 1869
(Co-served with {{ill|Kanda Mokkaku|ja|神田孟恪}}) | before = {{ill|Kanda Takahira|ja|神田孝平}} | after = {{ill|Ano Tadamichi|ja|阿野公誠}} }} {{S-end}} {{Normdaten}} {{People-stub}} [[Category:19th-century Japanese businessmen]] [[Category:Members of the House of Peers (Japan)]] [[Category:Imperial appointees to the House of Peers]] [[Category:Meiji-era politicians]] [[Category:Japanese diplomats]] [[Category:Japanese poets]] [[Category:Contributors to Japanese-Russian negotiations]] [[Category:People from Edo]] [[Category:1840 births]] [[Category:1899 deaths]] [[ja:丸山作楽]] {{draft categories| Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Maruyama Sakura]][[en:draft:Maruyama Sakura]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Maruyama Sakura|Maruyama Sakura]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Cite sources clearly|date=September 2015}} {{Politician | Native name = まるやま さくら | Image = Maruyama_Sakura.jpg | Image size = 200px | Country abbreviation = {{JPN}} | Birthdate = [[October 27]], [[1840]] | Birthplace = {{JPN}} Edo, Shiba Mita (present-day [[Minato, Tokyo|Minato, Tokyo]]) | Deathdate = {{Death date and age|1840|10|27|1899|8|19}} | Previous position = [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Ministry of Foreign Affairs]] [[Daisuke (official)|Daijō]] | Political party = [[Rikken Teiseitō]] | Titles and honors = [[Jūshi-i]]
[[Commemorative Medals#Promulgation of the Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Constitution Promulgation Medal]] | Office = [[House of Peers (Japan)|Member of the House of Peers]] | Start date = [[September 29]], [[1890]] | End date = [[August 19]], 1899 | Flag = JPN | Office2 = [[Gikanin|Gikan]] | Start date2 = [[June 12]], [[1890]] | End date2 = [[October 20]], 1890 | Flag2 = JPN }} '''Maruyama Sakura''' (丸山 作楽, {{ill|1840|ja|天保}} 11年 {{ill|October 3 (old calendar)|ja|10月3日 (旧暦)}} – [[August 19]], [[1899]]) was a [[Meiji period]] [[diplomat]], [[businessman]], and [[politician]]. He was also known by the names Masatora, Masamichi, and Sakura. His titles included Shinsūsho, Sokō, and Iwanoya. His wife's name was Umekichi (Okutsuki). == Career == Maruyama was born in Edo’s Shiba Mita district as the eldest son of Maruyama Masanao, a samurai of the {{ill|Shimabara Domain|ja|島原藩}}. During the late Edo period, he studied {{ill|Kokugaku|ja|国学}} of the {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}} school and became active in the royalist movement. In [[1869]] (Meiji 2), he became [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Daisuke]] and was dispatched to {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}} to engage in negotiations with Russia. Returning in [[1870]] (Meiji 3), he advocated an aggressive policy against Russian expansion, but his proposals were not adopted. He also supported {{ill|Sada Hakaru|ja|佐田白茅}}’s {{ill|Seikanron|ja|征韓論}} and urged its implementation, but this too was rejected. In November of the same year, he was reassigned to {{ill|Ken-Daisuke (Ministry of Foreign Affairs)|ja|外務権大丞}} due to the failure of the Karafuto negotiations. Maruyama, along with {{ill|Hatake Kiyotada|ja|畑経世}}, a samurai of the {{ill|Sasayama Domain|ja|篠山藩}}, attempted to carry out the rejected Seikanron through rebellion. However, in [[1871]] (Meiji 4), their plans were uncovered, and Maruyama was arrested on [[May 10]] while on duty at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. On [[May 29]], he was dismissed from his position as Ken-Daisuke. The incident, considered politically minor, led to his sentencing to life imprisonment. He was released in [[1880]] (Meiji 13) through amnesty. After his release, Maruyama opposed the {{ill|Freedom and People's Rights Movement|ja|自由民権運動}} by establishing the {{ill|Chūaisha|ja|忠愛社}} and publishing the newspaper {{ill|Meiji Nippo|ja|明治日報}}. In [[1882]] (Meiji 15), he co-founded the {{ill|Rikken Teiseitō|ja|立憲帝政党}} with {{ill|Fukuchi Gen'ichirō|ja|福地源一郎}} and worked as a conservative politician. In [[1890]] (Meiji 23), he was appointed {{ill|Gikanin|ja|元老院議官}} on [[June 12]] and became {{ill|Non-working official|ja|非職}} when the {{ill|Gikan|ja|元老院}} was abolished on [[October 20]]. He was subsequently named {{ill|Imperial appointee to the House of Peers|ja|貴族院勅選議員}} on [[September 29]], serving until his death on [[August 19]], [[1899]], due to valvular heart disease. Maruyama was also known as a {{ill|Manyōshū|ja|万葉集}}-style poet. == Honors == ; Rank * [[June 19]], [[1890]] (Meiji 23): [[Jūshi-i]]『官報』第2094号「叙任及辞令」1890年6月24日。 ; Medals * [[November 29]], [[1889]] (Meiji 22): {{ill|Constitution Promulgation Medal|ja|記念章#賞勲局所管の記念章}}『官報』第1935号「叙任及辞令」1889年12月9日。 == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Sources == * Shūgiin and Sangiin Editors, *{{ill|100-Year History of the Japanese Diet|ja|議会制度百年史}} - {{ill|House of Peers and Sangiin Member Directory|ja|貴族院・参議院議員名鑑}}*, 1990. * Maseo Watanabe and Junko Hirose, Editors, *{{ill|Records of Imperial Appointments|ja|国立公文書館所蔵 勅奏任官履歴原書}} Vol. 1*, Kashiwa Shobō, 1995. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Shimabara Samurai Residence Street|ja|島原武家屋敷街}} - Maruyama opened the private school {{ill|Shinsūsho|ja|神習処}} here in 1861. == External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/kensei/maruyamasakura National Diet Library Constitutional Archive Maruyama Sakura Documents (Microfilm: Private Collection)] {{S-start}} {{S-off}} {{Succession box | title = {{Flagicon|JPN}} {{ill|Lower Chamber Deputy Minister|ja|集議院下局次官}} | years = 1869
(Co-served with {{ill|Kanda Mokkaku|ja|神田孟恪}}) | before = {{ill|Kanda Takahira|ja|神田孝平}} | after = {{ill|Ano Tadamichi|ja|阿野公誠}} }} {{S-end}} {{Normdaten}} {{People-stub}} [[Category:19th-century He was also known by the names Masatora, Masamichi, and Sakura. His titles included Shinsūsho, Sokō, and Iwanoya. His wife's name was Umekichi (Okutsuki). == Career == Maruyama was born in Edo’s Shiba Mita district as the eldest son of Maruyama Masanao, a samurai of the {{ill|Shimabara Domain|ja|島原藩}}. During the late Edo period, he studied {{ill|Kokugaku|ja|国学}} of the {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}} school and became active in the royalist movement. In [[1869]] (Meiji 2), he became [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Daisuke]] and was dispatched to {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}} to engage in negotiations with Russia. Returning in [[1870]] (Meiji 3), he advocated an aggressive policy against Russian expansion, but his proposals were not adopted. He also supported {{ill|Sada Hakaru|ja|佐田白茅}}’s {{ill|Seikanron|ja|征韓論}} and urged its implementation, but this too was rejected. In November of the same year, he was reassigned to {{ill|Ken-Daisuke (Ministry of Foreign Affairs)|ja|外務権大丞}} due to the failure of the Karafuto negotiations. Maruyama, along with {{ill|Hatake Kiyotada|ja|畑経世}}, a samurai of the {{ill|Sasayama Domain|ja|篠山藩}}, attempted to carry out the rejected Seikanron through rebellion. However, in [[1871]] (Meiji 4), their plans were uncovered, and Maruyama was arrested on [[May 10]] while on duty at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. On [[May 29]], he was dismissed from his position as Ken-Daisuke. The incident, considered politically minor, led to his sentencing to life imprisonment. He was released in [[1880]] (Meiji 13) through amnesty. After his release, Maruyama opposed the {{ill|Freedom and People's Rights Movement|ja|自由民権運動}} by establishing the {{ill|Chūaisha|ja|忠愛社}} and publishing the newspaper {{ill|Meiji Nippo|ja|明治日報}}. In [[1882]] (Meiji 15), he co-founded the {{ill|Rikken Teiseitō|ja|立憲帝政党}} with {{ill|Fukuchi Gen'ichirō|ja|福地源一郎}} and worked as a conservative politician. In [[1890]] (Meiji 23), he was appointed {{ill|Gikanin|ja|元老院議官}} on [[June 12]] and became {{ill|Non-working official|ja|非職}} when the {{ill|Gikan|ja|元老院}} was abolished on [[October 20]]. He was subsequently named {{ill|Imperial appointee to the House of Peers|ja|貴族院勅選議員}} on [[September 29]], serving until his death on [[August 19]], [[1899]], due to valvular heart disease. Maruyama was also known as a {{ill|Manyōshū|ja|万葉集}}-style poet. == Honors == ; Rank * [[June 19]], [[1890]] (Meiji 23): [[Jūshi-i]]『官報』第2094号「叙任及辞令」1890年6月24日。 ; Medals * [[November 29]], [[1889]] (Meiji 22): {{ill|Constitution Promulgation Medal|ja|記念章#賞勲局所管の記念章}}『官報』第1935号「叙任及辞令」1889年12月9日。 == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Sources == * Shūgiin and Sangiin Editors, *{{ill|100-Year History of the Japanese Diet|ja|議会制度百年史}} - {{ill|House of Peers and Sangiin Member Directory|ja|貴族院・参議院議員名鑑}}*, 1990. * Maseo Watanabe and Junko Hirose, Editors, *{{ill|Records of Imperial Appointments|ja|国立公文書館所蔵 勅奏任官履歴原書}} Vol. 1*, Kashiwa Shobō, 1995. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Shimabara Samurai Residence Street|ja|島原武家屋敷街}} - Maruyama opened the private school {{ill|Shinsūsho|ja|神習処}} here in 1861. == External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/kensei/maruyamasakura National Diet Library Constitutional Archive Maruyama Sakura Documents (Microfilm: Private Collection)] {{S-start}} {{S-off}} {{Succession box | title = {{Flagicon|JPN}} {{ill|Lower Chamber Deputy Minister|ja|集議院下局次官}} | years = 1869
(Co-served with {{ill|Kanda Mokkaku|ja|神田孟恪}}) | before = {{ill|Kanda Takahira|ja|神田孝平}} | after = {{ill|Ano Tadamichi|ja|阿野公誠}} }} {{S-end}} {{Normdaten}} {{People-stub}} [[Category:19th-century [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Cite sources clearly|date=September 2015}} {{Politician | Native name = まるやま さくら | Image = Maruyama_Sakura.jpg | Image size = 200px | Country abbreviation = {{JPN}} | Birthdate = [[October 27]], [[1840]] | Birthplace = {{JPN}} Edo, Shiba Mita (present-day [[Minato, Tokyo|Minato, Tokyo]]) | Deathdate = {{Death date and age|1840|10|27|1899|8|19}} | Previous position = [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Ministry of Foreign Affairs]] [[Daisuke (official)|Daijō]] | Political party = [[Rikken Teiseitō]] | Titles and honors = [[Jūshi-i]]
[[Commemorative Medals#Promulgation of the Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Constitution Promulgation Medal]] | Office = [[House of Peers (Japan)|Member of the House of Peers]] | Start date = [[September 29]], [[1890]] | End date = [[August 19]], 1899 | Flag = JPN | Office2 = [[Gikanin|Gikan]] | Start date2 = [[June 12]], [[1890]] | End date2 = [[October 20]], 1890 | Flag2 = JPN }} '''Maruyama Sakura''' (丸山 作楽, {{ill|1840|ja|天保}} 11年 {{ill|October 3 (old calendar)|ja|10月3日 (旧暦)}} – [[August 19]], [[1899]]) was a [[Meiji period]] [[diplomat]], [[businessman]], and [[politician]]. He was also known by the names Masatora, Masamichi, and Sakura. His titles included Shinsūsho, Sokō, and Iwanoya. His wife's name was Umekichi (Okutsuki). == Career == Maruyama was born in Edo’s Shiba Mita district as the eldest son of Maruyama Masanao, a samurai of the {{ill|Shimabara Domain|ja|島原藩}}. During the late Edo period, he studied {{ill|Kokugaku|ja|国学}} of the {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}} school and became active in the royalist movement. In [[1869]] (Meiji 2), he became [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Daisuke]] and was dispatched to {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}} to engage in negotiations with Russia. Returning in [[1870]] (Meiji 3), he advocated an aggressive policy against Russian expansion, but his proposals were not adopted. He also supported {{ill|Sada Hakaru|ja|佐田白茅}}’s {{ill|Seikanron|ja|征韓論}} and urged its implementation, but this too was rejected. In November of the same year, he was reassigned to {{ill|Ken-Daisuke (Ministry of Foreign Affairs)|ja|外務権大丞}} due to the failure of the Karafuto negotiations. Maruyama, along with {{ill|Hatake Kiyotada|ja|畑経世}}, a samurai of the {{ill|Sasayama Domain|ja|篠山藩}}, attempted to carry out the rejected Seikanron through rebellion. However, in [[1871]] (Meiji 4), their plans were uncovered, and Maruyama was arrested on [[May 10]] while on duty at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. On [[May 29]], he was dismissed from his position as Ken-Daisuke. The incident, considered politically minor, led to his sentencing to life imprisonment. He was released in [[1880]] (Meiji 13) through amnesty. After his release, Maruyama opposed the {{ill|Freedom and People's Rights Movement|ja|自由民権運動}} by establishing the {{ill|Chūaisha|ja|忠愛社}} and publishing the newspaper {{ill|Meiji Nippo|ja|明治日報}}. In [[1882]] (Meiji 15), he co-founded the {{ill|Rikken Teiseitō|ja|立憲帝政党}} with {{ill|Fukuchi Gen'ichirō|ja|福地源一郎}} and worked as a conservative politician. In [[1890]] (Meiji 23), he was appointed {{ill|Gikanin|ja|元老院議官}} on [[June 12]] and became {{ill|Non-working official|ja|非職}} when the {{ill|Gikan|ja|元老院}} was abolished on [[October 20]]. He was subsequently named {{ill|Imperial appointee to the House of Peers|ja|貴族院勅選議員}} on [[September 29]], serving until his death on [[August 19]], [[1899]], due to valvular heart disease. Maruyama was also known as a {{ill|Manyōshū|ja|万葉集}}-style poet. == Honors == ; Rank * [[June 19]], [[1890]] (Meiji 23): [[Jūshi-i]]『官報』第2094号「叙任及辞令」1890年6月24日。 ; Medals * [[November 29]], [[1889]] (Meiji 22): {{ill|Constitution Promulgation Medal|ja|記念章#賞勲局所管の記念章}}『官報』第1935号「叙任及辞令」1889年12月9日。 == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Sources == * Shūgiin and Sangiin Editors, *{{ill|100-Year History of the Japanese Diet|ja|議会制度百年史}} - {{ill|House of Peers and Sangiin Member Directory|ja|貴族院・参議院議員名鑑}}*, 1990. * Maseo Watanabe and Junko Hirose, Editors, *{{ill|Records of Imperial Appointments|ja|国立公文書館所蔵 勅奏任官履歴原書}} Vol. 1*, Kashiwa Shobō, 1995. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Shimabara Samurai Residence Street|ja|島原武家屋敷街}} - Maruyama opened the private school {{ill|Shinsūsho|ja|神習処}} here in 1861. == External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/kensei/maruyamasakura National Diet Library Constitutional Archive Maruyama Sakura Documents (Microfilm: Private Collection)] {{S-start}} {{S-off}} {{Succession box | title = {{Flagicon|JPN}} {{ill|Lower Chamber Deputy Minister|ja|集議院下局次官}} | years = 1869
(Co-served with {{ill|Kanda Mokkaku|ja|神田孟恪}}) | before = {{ill|Kanda Takahira|ja|神田孝平}} | after = {{ill|Ano Tadamichi|ja|阿野公誠}} }} {{S-end}} {{Normdaten}} {{People-stub}} [[Category:19th-century Japanese businessmen]] [[Category:Members of the House of Peers (Japan)]] [[Category:Imperial appointees to the House of Peers]] [[Category:Meiji-era politicians]] [[Category:Japanese diplomats]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Cite sources clearly|date=September 2015}} {{Politician | Native name = まるやま さくら | Image = Maruyama_Sakura.jpg | Image size = 200px | Country abbreviation = {{JPN}} | Birthdate = [[October 27]], [[1840]] | Birthplace = {{JPN}} Edo, Shiba Mita (present-day [[Minato, Tokyo|Minato, Tokyo]]) | Deathdate = {{Death date and age|1840|10|27|1899|8|19}} | Previous position = [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Ministry of Foreign Affairs]] [[Daisuke (official)|Daijō]] | Political party = [[Rikken Teiseitō]] | Titles and honors = [[Jūshi-i]]
[[Commemorative Medals#Promulgation of the Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Constitution Promulgation Medal]] | Office = [[House of Peers (Japan)|Member of the House of Peers]] | Start date = [[September 29]], [[1890]] | End date = [[August 19]], 1899 | Flag = JPN | Office2 = [[Gikanin|Gikan]] | Start date2 = [[June 12]], [[1890]] | End date2 = [[October 20]], 1890 | Flag2 = JPN }} '''Maruyama Sakura''' (丸山 作楽, {{ill|1840|ja|天保}} 11年 {{ill|October 3 (old calendar)|ja|10月3日 (旧暦)}} – [[August 19]], [[1899]]) was a [[Meiji period]] [[diplomat]], [[businessman]], and [[politician]]. He was also known by the names Masatora, Masamichi, and Sakura. His titles included Shinsūsho, Sokō, and Iwanoya. His wife's name was Umekichi (Okutsuki). == Career == Maruyama was born in Edo’s Shiba Mita district as the eldest son of Maruyama Masanao, a samurai of the {{ill|Shimabara Domain|ja|島原藩}}. During the late Edo period, he studied {{ill|Kokugaku|ja|国学}} of the {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}} school and became active in the royalist movement. In [[1869]] (Meiji 2), he became [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Daisuke]] and was dispatched to {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}} to engage in negotiations with Russia. Returning in [[1870]] (Meiji 3), he advocated an aggressive policy against Russian expansion, but his proposals were not adopted. He also supported {{ill|Sada Hakaru|ja|佐田白茅}}’s {{ill|Seikanron|ja|征韓論}} and urged its implementation, but this too was rejected. In November of the same year, he was reassigned to {{ill|Ken-Daisuke (Ministry of Foreign Affairs)|ja|外務権大丞}} due to the failure of the Karafuto negotiations. Maruyama, along with {{ill|Hatake Kiyotada|ja|畑経世}}, a samurai of the {{ill|Sasayama Domain|ja|篠山藩}}, attempted to carry out the rejected Seikanron through rebellion. However, in [[1871]] (Meiji 4), their plans were uncovered, and Maruyama was arrested on [[May 10]] while on duty at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. On [[May 29]], he was dismissed from his position as Ken-Daisuke. The incident, considered politically minor, led to his sentencing to life imprisonment. He was released in [[1880]] (Meiji 13) through amnesty. After his release, Maruyama opposed the {{ill|Freedom and People's Rights Movement|ja|自由民権運動}} by establishing the {{ill|Chūaisha|ja|忠愛社}} and publishing the newspaper {{ill|Meiji Nippo|ja|明治日報}}. In [[1882]] (Meiji 15), he co-founded the {{ill|Rikken Teiseitō|ja|立憲帝政党}} with {{ill|Fukuchi Gen'ichirō|ja|福地源一郎}} and worked as a conservative politician. In [[1890]] (Meiji 23), he was appointed {{ill|Gikanin|ja|元老院議官}} on [[June 12]] and became {{ill|Non-working official|ja|非職}} when the {{ill|Gikan|ja|元老院}} was abolished on [[October 20]]. He was subsequently named {{ill|Imperial appointee to the House of Peers|ja|貴族院勅選議員}} on [[September 29]], serving until his death on [[August 19]], [[1899]], due to valvular heart disease. Maruyama was also known as a {{ill|Manyōshū|ja|万葉集}}-style poet. == Honors == ; Rank * [[June 19]], [[1890]] (Meiji 23): [[Jūshi-i]]『官報』第2094号「叙任及辞令」1890年6月24日。 ; Medals * [[November 29]], [[1889]] (Meiji 22): {{ill|Constitution Promulgation Medal|ja|記念章#賞勲局所管の記念章}}『官報』第1935号「叙任及辞令」1889年12月9日。 == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Sources == * Shūgiin and Sangiin Editors, *{{ill|100-Year History of the Japanese Diet|ja|議会制度百年史}} - {{ill|House of Peers and Sangiin Member Directory|ja|貴族院・参議院議員名鑑}}*, 1990. * Maseo Watanabe and Junko Hirose, Editors, *{{ill|Records of Imperial Appointments|ja|国立公文書館所蔵 勅奏任官履歴原書}} Vol. 1*, Kashiwa Shobō, 1995. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Shimabara Samurai Residence Street|ja|島原武家屋敷街}} - Maruyama opened the private school {{ill|Shinsūsho|ja|神習処}} here in 1861. == External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/kensei/maruyamasakura National Diet Library Constitutional Archive Maruyama Sakura Documents (Microfilm: Private Collection)] {{S-start}} {{S-off}} {{Succession box | title = {{Flagicon|JPN}} {{ill|Lower Chamber Deputy Minister|ja|集議院下局次官}} | years = 1869
(Co-served with {{ill|Kanda Mokkaku|ja|神田孟恪}}) | before = {{ill|Kanda Takahira|ja|神田孝平}} | after = {{ill|Ano Tadamichi|ja|阿野公誠}} }} {{S-end}} {{Normdaten}} {{People-stub}} [[Category:19th-century In November of the same year, he was reassigned to {{ill|Ken-Daisuke (Ministry of Foreign Affairs)|ja|外務権大丞}} due to the failure of the Karafuto negotiations. Maruyama, along with {{ill|Hatake Kiyotada|ja|畑経世}}, a samurai of the {{ill|Sasayama Domain|ja|篠山藩}}, attempted to carry out the rejected Seikanron through rebellion. However, in [[1871]] (Meiji 4), their plans were uncovered, and Maruyama was arrested on [[May 10]] while on duty at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. On [[May 29]], he was dismissed from his position as Ken-Daisuke. The incident, considered politically minor, led to his sentencing to life imprisonment. He was released in [[1880]] (Meiji 13) through amnesty. After his release, Maruyama opposed the {{ill|Freedom and People's Rights Movement|ja|自由民権運動}} by establishing the {{ill|Chūaisha|ja|忠愛社}} and publishing the newspaper {{ill|Meiji Nippo|ja|明治日報}}. In [[1882]] (Meiji 15), he co-founded the {{ill|Rikken Teiseitō|ja|立憲帝政党}} with {{ill|Fukuchi Gen'ichirō|ja|福地源一郎}} and worked as a conservative politician. In [[1890]] (Meiji 23), he was appointed {{ill|Gikanin|ja|元老院議官}} on [[June 12]] and became {{ill|Non-working official|ja|非職}} when the {{ill|Gikan|ja|元老院}} was abolished on [[October 20]]. He was subsequently named {{ill|Imperial appointee to the House of Peers|ja|貴族院勅選議員}} on [[September 29]], serving until his death on [[August 19]], [[1899]], due to valvular heart disease. Maruyama was also known as a {{ill|Manyōshū|ja|万葉集}}-style poet. == Honors == ; Rank * [[June 19]], [[1890]] (Meiji 23): [[Jūshi-i]]『官報』第2094号「叙任及辞令」1890年6月24日。 ; Medals * [[November 29]], [[1889]] (Meiji 22): {{ill|Constitution Promulgation Medal|ja|記念章#賞勲局所管の記念章}}『官報』第1935号「叙任及辞令」1889年12月9日。 == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Sources == * Shūgiin and Sangiin Editors, *{{ill|100-Year History of the Japanese Diet|ja|議会制度百年史}} - {{ill|House of Peers and Sangiin Member Directory|ja|貴族院・参議院議員名鑑}}*, 1990. * Maseo Watanabe and Junko Hirose, Editors, *{{ill|Records of Imperial Appointments|ja|国立公文書館所蔵 勅奏任官履歴原書}} Vol. 1*, Kashiwa Shobō, 1995. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Shimabara Samurai Residence Street|ja|島原武家屋敷街}} - Maruyama opened the private school {{ill|Shinsūsho|ja|神習処}} here in 1861. == External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/kensei/maruyamasakura National Diet Library Constitutional Archive Maruyama Sakura Documents (Microfilm: Private Collection)] {{S-start}} {{S-off}} {{Succession box | title = {{Flagicon|JPN}} {{ill|Lower Chamber Deputy Minister|ja|集議院下局次官}} | years = 1869
(Co-served with {{ill|Kanda Mokkaku|ja|神田孟恪}}) | before = {{ill|Kanda Takahira|ja|神田孝平}} | after = {{ill|Ano Tadamichi|ja|阿野公誠}} }} {{S-end}} {{Normdaten}} {{People-stub}} [[Category:19th-century [[Commemorative Medals#Promulgation of the Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Constitution Promulgation Medal]] | Office = [[House of Peers (Japan)|Member of the House of Peers]] | Start date = [[September 29]], [[1890]] | End date = [[August 19]], 1899 | Flag = JPN | Office2 = [[Gikanin|Gikan]] | Start date2 = [[June 12]], [[1890]] | End date2 = [[October 20]], 1890 | Flag2 = JPN }} '''Maruyama Sakura''' (丸山 作楽, {{ill|1840|ja|天保}} 11年 {{ill|October 3 (old calendar)|ja|10月3日 (旧暦)}} – [[August 19]], [[1899]]) was a [[Meiji period]] [[diplomat]], [[businessman]], and [[politician]]. He was also known by the names Masatora, Masamichi, and Sakura. His titles included Shinsūsho, Sokō, and Iwanoya. His wife's name was Umekichi (Okutsuki). == Career == Maruyama was born in Edo’s Shiba Mita district as the eldest son of Maruyama Masanao, a samurai of the {{ill|Shimabara Domain|ja|島原藩}}. During the late Edo period, he studied {{ill|Kokugaku|ja|国学}} of the {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}} school and became active in the royalist movement. In [[1869]] (Meiji 2), he became [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Daisuke]] and was dispatched to {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}} to engage in negotiations with Russia. Returning in [[1870]] (Meiji 3), he advocated an aggressive policy against Russian expansion, but his proposals were not adopted. He also supported {{ill|Sada Hakaru|ja|佐田白茅}}’s {{ill|Seikanron|ja|征韓論}} and urged its implementation, but this too was rejected. In November of the same year, he was reassigned to {{ill|Ken-Daisuke (Ministry of Foreign Affairs)|ja|外務権大丞}} due to the failure of the Karafuto negotiations. Maruyama, along with {{ill|Hatake Kiyotada|ja|畑経世}}, a samurai of the {{ill|Sasayama Domain|ja|篠山藩}}, attempted to carry out the rejected Seikanron through rebellion. However, in [[1871]] (Meiji 4), their plans were uncovered, and Maruyama was arrested on [[May 10]] while on duty at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. On [[May 29]], he was dismissed from his position as Ken-Daisuke. The incident, considered politically minor, led to his sentencing to life imprisonment. He was released in [[1880]] (Meiji 13) through amnesty. After his release, Maruyama opposed the {{ill|Freedom and People's Rights Movement|ja|自由民権運動}} by establishing the {{ill|Chūaisha|ja|忠愛社}} and publishing the newspaper {{ill|Meiji Nippo|ja|明治日報}}. In [[1882]] (Meiji 15), he co-founded the {{ill|Rikken Teiseitō|ja|立憲帝政党}} with {{ill|Fukuchi Gen'ichirō|ja|福地源一郎}} and worked as a conservative politician. In [[1890]] (Meiji 23), he was appointed {{ill|Gikanin|ja|元老院議官}} on [[June 12]] and became {{ill|Non-working official|ja|非職}} when the {{ill|Gikan|ja|元老院}} was abolished on [[October 20]]. He was subsequently named {{ill|Imperial appointee to the House of Peers|ja|貴族院勅選議員}} on [[September 29]], serving until his death on [[August 19]], [[1899]], due to valvular heart disease. Maruyama was also known as a {{ill|Manyōshū|ja|万葉集}}-style poet. == Honors == ; Rank * [[June 19]], [[1890]] (Meiji 23): [[Jūshi-i]]『官報』第2094号「叙任及辞令」1890年6月24日。 ; Medals * [[November 29]], [[1889]] (Meiji 22): {{ill|Constitution Promulgation Medal|ja|記念章#賞勲局所管の記念章}}『官報』第1935号「叙任及辞令」1889年12月9日。 == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Sources == * Shūgiin and Sangiin Editors, *{{ill|100-Year History of the Japanese Diet|ja|議会制度百年史}} - {{ill|House of Peers and Sangiin Member Directory|ja|貴族院・参議院議員名鑑}}*, 1990. * Maseo Watanabe and Junko Hirose, Editors, *{{ill|Records of Imperial Appointments|ja|国立公文書館所蔵 勅奏任官履歴原書}} Vol. 1*, Kashiwa Shobō, 1995. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Shimabara Samurai Residence Street|ja|島原武家屋敷街}} - Maruyama opened the private school {{ill|Shinsūsho|ja|神習処}} here in 1861. == External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/kensei/maruyamasakura National Diet Library Constitutional Archive Maruyama Sakura Documents (Microfilm: Private Collection)] {{S-start}} {{S-off}} {{Succession box | title = {{Flagicon|JPN}} {{ill|Lower Chamber Deputy Minister|ja|集議院下局次官}} | years = 1869

[[Commemorative Medals#Promulgation of the Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Constitution Promulgation Medal]] | Office = [[House of Peers (Japan)|Member of the House of Peers]] | Start date = [[September 29]], [[1890]] | End date = [[August 19]], 1899 | Flag = JPN | Office2 = [[Gikanin|Gikan]] | Start date2 = [[June 12]], [[1890]] | End date2 = [[October 20]], 1890 | Flag2 = JPN }} '''Maruyama Sakura''' (丸山 作楽, {{ill|1840|ja|天保}} 11年 {{ill|October 3 (old calendar)|ja|10月3日 (旧暦)}} – [[August 19]], [[1899]]) was a [[Meiji period]] [[diplomat]], [[businessman]], and [[politician]]. He was also known by the names Masatora, Masamichi, and Sakura. His titles included Shinsūsho, Sokō, and Iwanoya. His wife's name was Umekichi (Okutsuki). == Career == Maruyama was born in Edo’s Shiba Mita district as the eldest son of Maruyama Masanao, a samurai of the {{ill|Shimabara Domain|ja|島原藩}}. During the late Edo period, he studied {{ill|Kokugaku|ja|国学}} of the {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}} school and became active in the royalist movement. In [[1869]] (Meiji 2), he became [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Daisuke]] and was dispatched to {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}} to engage in negotiations with Russia. Returning in [[1870]] (Meiji 3), he advocated an aggressive policy against Russian expansion, but his proposals were not adopted. He also supported {{ill|Sada Hakaru|ja|佐田白茅}}’s {{ill|Seikanron|ja|征韓論}} and urged its implementation, but this too was rejected. In November of the same year, he was reassigned to {{ill|Ken-Daisuke (Ministry of Foreign Affairs)|ja|外務権大丞}} due to the failure of the Karafuto negotiations. Maruyama, along with {{ill|Hatake Kiyotada|ja|畑経世}}, a samurai of the {{ill|Sasayama Domain|ja|篠山藩}}, attempted to carry out the rejected Seikanron through rebellion. However, in [[1871]] (Meiji 4), their plans were uncovered, and Maruyama was arrested on [[May 10]] while on duty at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. On [[May 29]], he was dismissed from his position as Ken-Daisuke. The incident, considered politically minor, led to his sentencing to life imprisonment. He was released in [[1880]] (Meiji 13) through amnesty. After his release, Maruyama opposed the {{ill|Freedom and People's Rights Movement|ja|自由民権運動}} by establishing the {{ill|Chūaisha|ja|忠愛社}} and publishing the newspaper {{ill|Meiji Nippo|ja|明治日報}}. In [[1882]] (Meiji 15), he co-founded the {{ill|Rikken Teiseitō|ja|立憲帝政党}} with {{ill|Fukuchi Gen'ichirō|ja|福地源一郎}} and worked as a conservative politician. In [[1890]] (Meiji 23), he was appointed {{ill|Gikanin|ja|元老院議官}} on [[June 12]] and became {{ill|Non-working official|ja|非職}} when the {{ill|Gikan|ja|元老院}} was abolished on [[October 20]]. He was subsequently named {{ill|Imperial appointee to the House of Peers|ja|貴族院勅選議員}} on [[September 29]], serving until his death on [[August 19]], [[1899]], due to valvular heart disease. Maruyama was also known as a {{ill|Manyōshū|ja|万葉集}}-style poet. == Honors == ; Rank * [[June 19]], [[1890]] (Meiji 23): [[Jūshi-i]]『官報』第2094号「叙任及辞令」1890年6月24日。 ; Medals * [[November 29]], [[1889]] (Meiji 22): {{ill|Constitution Promulgation Medal|ja|記念章#賞勲局所管の記念章}}『官報』第1935号「叙任及辞令」1889年12月9日。 == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Sources == * Shūgiin and Sangiin Editors, *{{ill|100-Year History of the Japanese Diet|ja|議会制度百年史}} - {{ill|House of Peers and Sangiin Member Directory|ja|貴族院・参議院議員名鑑}}*, 1990. * Maseo Watanabe and Junko Hirose, Editors, *{{ill|Records of Imperial Appointments|ja|国立公文書館所蔵 勅奏任官履歴原書}} Vol. 1*, Kashiwa Shobō, 1995. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Shimabara Samurai Residence Street|ja|島原武家屋敷街}} - Maruyama opened the private school {{ill|Shinsūsho|ja|神習処}} here in 1861. == External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/kensei/maruyamasakura National Diet Library Constitutional Archive Maruyama Sakura Documents (Microfilm: Private Collection)] {{S-start}} {{S-off}} {{Succession box | title = {{Flagicon|JPN}} {{ill|Lower Chamber Deputy Minister|ja|集議院下局次官}} | years = 1869
(Co-served with {{ill|Kanda Mokkaku|ja|神田孟恪}}) | before = {{ill|Kanda Takahira|ja|神田孝平}} | after = {{ill|Ano Tadamichi|ja|阿野公誠}} }} {{S-end}} {{Normdaten}} {{People-stub}} [[Category:19th-century Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Maruyama Sakura]][[en:draft:Maruyama Sakura]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Maruyama Sakura|Maruyama Sakura]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Cite sources clearly|date=September 2015}} {{Politician | Native name = まるやま さくら | image = Maruyama_Sakura.jpg | image size = 200px | Country abbreviation = {{JPN}} | Birthdate = [[October 27]], [[1840]] | Birthplace = {{JPN}} Edo, Shiba Mita (present-day [[Minato, Tokyo|Minato, Tokyo]]) | Deathdate = {{Death date and age|1840|10|27|1899|8|19}} | Previous position = [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Ministry of Foreign Affairs]] [[Daisuke (official)|Daijō]] | Political party = [[Rikken Teiseitō]] | Titles and honors = [[Jūshi-i]]
[[Commemorative Medals#Promulgation of the Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Constitution Promulgation Medal]] | Office = [[House of Peers (Japan)|Member of the House of Peers]] | Start date = [[September 29]], [[1890]] | End date = [[August 19]], 1899 | Flag = JPN | Office2 = [[Gikanin|Gikan]] | Start date2 = [[June 12]], [[1890]] | End date2 = [[October 20]], 1890 | Flag2 = JPN }} '''Maruyama Sakura''' (丸山 作楽, {{ill|1840|ja|天保}} 11年 {{ill|October 3 (old calendar)|ja|10月3日 (旧暦)}} – [[August 19]], [[1899]]) was a [[Meiji period]] [[diplomat]], [[businessman]], and [[politician]]. He was also known by the names Masatora, Masamichi, and Sakura. His titles included Shinsūsho, Sokō, and Iwanoya. His wife's name was Umekichi (Okutsuki). == Career == Maruyama was born in Edo’s Shiba Mita district as the eldest son of Maruyama Masanao, a samurai of the {{ill|Shimabara Domain|ja|島原藩}}. During the late Edo period, he studied {{ill|Kokugaku|ja|国学}} of the {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}} school and became active in the royalist movement. In [[1869]] (Meiji 2), he became [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Daisuke]] and was dispatched to {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}} to engage in negotiations with Russia. Returning in [[1870]] (Meiji 3), he advocated an aggressive policy against Russian expansion, but his proposals were not adopted. He also supported {{ill|Sada Hakaru|ja|佐田白茅}}’s {{ill|Seikanron|ja|征韓論}} and urged its implementation, but this too was rejected. In November of the same year, he was reassigned to {{ill|Ken-Daisuke (Ministry of Foreign Affairs)|ja|外務権大丞}} due to the failure of the Karafuto negotiations. Maruyama, along with {{ill|Hatake Kiyotada|ja|畑経世}}, a samurai of the {{ill|Sasayama Domain|ja|篠山藩}}, attempted to carry out the rejected Seikanron through rebellion. However, in [[1871]] (Meiji 4), their plans were uncovered, and Maruyama was arrested on [[May 10]] while on duty at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. On [[May 29]], he was dismissed from his position as Ken-Daisuke. The incident, considered politically minor, led to his sentencing to life imprisonment. He was released in [[1880]] (Meiji 13) through amnesty. After his release, Maruyama opposed the {{ill|Freedom and People's Rights Movement|ja|自由民権運動}} by establishing the {{ill|Chūaisha|ja|忠愛社}} and publishing the newspaper {{ill|Meiji Nippo|ja|明治日報}}. In [[1882]] (Meiji 15), he co-founded the {{ill|Rikken Teiseitō|ja|立憲帝政党}} with {{ill|Fukuchi Gen'ichirō|ja|福地源一郎}} and worked as a conservative politician. In [[1890]] (Meiji 23), he was appointed {{ill|Gikanin|ja|元老院議官}} on [[June 12]] and became {{ill|Non-working official|ja|非職}} when the {{ill|Gikan|ja|元老院}} was abolished on [[October 20]]. He was subsequently named {{ill|Imperial appointee to the House of Peers|ja|貴族院勅選議員}} on [[September 29]], serving until his death on [[August 19]], [[1899]], due to valvular heart disease. Maruyama was also known as a {{ill|Manyōshū|ja|万葉集}}-style poet. == Honors == ; Rank * [[June 19]], [[1890]] (Meiji 23): [[Jūshi-i]]『官報』第2094号「叙任及辞令」1890年6月24日。 ; Medals * [[November 29]], [[1889]] (Meiji 22): {{ill|Constitution Promulgation Medal|ja|記念章#賞勲局所管の記念章}}『官報』第1935号「叙任及辞令」1889年12月9日。 == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Sources == * Shūgiin and Sangiin Editors, *{{ill|100-Year History of the Japanese Diet|ja|議会制度百年史}} - {{ill|House of Peers and Sangiin Member Directory|ja|貴族院・参議院議員名鑑}}*, 1990. * Maseo Watanabe and Junko Hirose, Editors, *{{ill|Records of Imperial Appointments|ja|国立公文書館所蔵 勅奏任官履歴原書}} Vol. 1*, Kashiwa Shobō, 1995. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Shimabara Samurai Residence Street|ja|島原武家屋敷街}} - Maruyama opened the private school {{ill|Shinsūsho|ja|神習処}} here in 1861. == External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/kensei/maruyamasakura National Diet Library Constitutional Archive Maruyama Sakura Documents (Microfilm: Private Collection)] {{S-start}} {{S-off}} {{Succession box | title = {{Flagicon|JPN}} {{ill|Lower Chamber Deputy Minister|ja|集議院下局次官}} | years = 1869
(Co-served with {{ill|Kanda Mokkaku|ja|神田孟恪}}) | before = {{ill|Kanda Takahira|ja|神田孝平}} | after = {{ill|Ano Tadamichi|ja|阿野公誠}} }} {{S-end}} {{Normdaten}} {{People-stub}} [[Category:19th-century Japanese businessmen]] [[Category:Members of the House of Peers (Japan)]] [[Category:Imperial appointees to the House of Peers]] [[Category:Meiji-era politicians]] [[Category:Japanese diplomats]] Maruyama was also known as a {{ill|Manyōshū|ja|万葉集}}-style poet. == Honors == ; Rank * [[June 19]], [[1890]] (Meiji 23): [[Jūshi-i]]『官報』第2094号「叙任及辞令」1890年6月24日。 ; Medals * [[November 29]], [[1889]] (Meiji 22): {{ill|Constitution Promulgation Medal|ja|記念章#賞勲局所管の記念章}}『官報』第1935号「叙任及辞令」1889年12月9日。 == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Sources == * Shūgiin and Sangiin Editors, *{{ill|100-Year History of the Japanese Diet|ja|議会制度百年史}} - {{ill|House of Peers and Sangiin Member Directory|ja|貴族院・参議院議員名鑑}}*, 1990. * Maseo Watanabe and Junko Hirose, Editors, *{{ill|Records of Imperial Appointments|ja|国立公文書館所蔵 勅奏任官履歴原書}} Vol. 1*, Kashiwa Shobō, 1995. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Shimabara Samurai Residence Street|ja|島原武家屋敷街}} - Maruyama opened the private school {{ill|Shinsūsho|ja|神習処}} here in 1861. == External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/kensei/maruyamasakura National Diet Library Constitutional Archive Maruyama Sakura Documents (Microfilm: Private Collection)] {{S-start}} {{S-off}} {{Succession box | title = {{Flagicon|JPN}} {{ill|Lower Chamber Deputy Minister|ja|集議院下局次官}} | years = 1869

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Maruyama Sakura]][[en:draft:Maruyama Sakura]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Maruyama Sakura|Maruyama Sakura]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Cite sources clearly|date=September 2015}} {{Politician | Native name = まるやま さくら | image = Maruyama_Sakura.jpg | image size = 200px | Country abbreviation = {{JPN}} | Birthdate = [[October 27]], [[1840]] | Birthplace = {{JPN}} Edo, Shiba Mita (present-day [[Minato, Tokyo|Minato, Tokyo]]) | Deathdate = {{Death date and age|1840|10|27|1899|8|19}} | Previous position = [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Ministry of Foreign Affairs]] [[Daisuke (official)|Daijō]] | Political party = [[Rikken Teiseitō]] | Titles and honors = [[Jūshi-i]]
[[Commemorative Medals#Promulgation of the Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Constitution Promulgation Medal]] | Office = [[House of Peers (Japan)|Member of the House of Peers]] | Start date = [[September 29]], [[1890]] | End date = [[August 19]], 1899 | Flag = JPN | Office2 = [[Gikanin|Gikan]] | Start date2 = [[June 12]], [[1890]] | End date2 = [[October 20]], 1890 | Flag2 = JPN }} '''Maruyama Sakura''' (丸山 作楽, {{ill|1840|ja|天保}} 11年 {{ill|October 3 (old calendar)|ja|10月3日 (旧暦)}} – [[August 19]], [[1899]]) was a [[Meiji period]] [[diplomat]], [[businessman]], and [[politician]]. He was also known by the names Masatora, Masamichi, and Sakura. His titles included Shinsūsho, Sokō, and Iwanoya. His wife's name was Umekichi (Okutsuki). == Career == Maruyama was born in Edo’s Shiba Mita district as the eldest son of Maruyama Masanao, a samurai of the {{ill|Shimabara Domain|ja|島原藩}}. During the late Edo period, he studied {{ill|Kokugaku|ja|国学}} of the {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}} school and became active in the royalist movement. In [[1869]] (Meiji 2), he became [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Daisuke]] and was dispatched to {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}} to engage in negotiations with Russia. Returning in [[1870]] (Meiji 3), he advocated an aggressive policy against Russian expansion, but his proposals were not adopted. He also supported {{ill|Sada Hakaru|ja|佐田白茅}}’s {{ill|Seikanron|ja|征韓論}} and urged its implementation, but this too was rejected. In November of the same year, he was reassigned to {{ill|Ken-Daisuke (Ministry of Foreign Affairs)|ja|外務権大丞}} due to the failure of the Karafuto negotiations. Maruyama, along with {{ill|Hatake Kiyotada|ja|畑経世}}, a samurai of the {{ill|Sasayama Domain|ja|篠山藩}}, attempted to carry out the rejected Seikanron through rebellion. However, in [[1871]] (Meiji 4), their plans were uncovered, and Maruyama was arrested on [[May 10]] while on duty at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. On [[May 29]], he was dismissed from his position as Ken-Daisuke. The incident, considered politically minor, led to his sentencing to life imprisonment. He was released in [[1880]] (Meiji 13) through amnesty. After his release, Maruyama opposed the {{ill|Freedom and People's Rights Movement|ja|自由民権運動}} by establishing the {{ill|Chūaisha|ja|忠愛社}} and publishing the newspaper {{ill|Meiji Nippo|ja|明治日報}}. In [[1882]] (Meiji 15), he co-founded the {{ill|Rikken Teiseitō|ja|立憲帝政党}} with {{ill|Fukuchi Gen'ichirō|ja|福地源一郎}} and worked as a conservative politician. In [[1890]] (Meiji 23), he was appointed {{ill|Gikanin|ja|元老院議官}} on [[June 12]] and became {{ill|Non-working official|ja|非職}} when the {{ill|Gikan|ja|元老院}} was abolished on [[October 20]]. He was subsequently named {{ill|Imperial appointee to the House of Peers|ja|貴族院勅選議員}} on [[September 29]], serving until his death on [[August 19]], [[1899]], due to valvular heart disease. Maruyama was also known as a {{ill|Manyōshū|ja|万葉集}}-style poet. == Honors == ; Rank * [[June 19]], [[1890]] (Meiji 23): [[Jūshi-i]]『官報』第2094号「叙任及辞令」1890年6月24日。 ; Medals * [[November 29]], [[1889]] (Meiji 22): {{ill|Constitution Promulgation Medal|ja|記念章#賞勲局所管の記念章}}『官報』第1935号「叙任及辞令」1889年12月9日。 == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Sources == * Shūgiin and Sangiin Editors, *{{ill|100-Year History of the Japanese Diet|ja|議会制度百年史}} - {{ill|House of Peers and Sangiin Member Directory|ja|貴族院・参議院議員名鑑}}*, 1990. * Maseo Watanabe and Junko Hirose, Editors, *{{ill|Records of Imperial Appointments|ja|国立公文書館所蔵 勅奏任官履歴原書}} Vol. 1*, Kashiwa Shobō, 1995. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Shimabara Samurai Residence Street|ja|島原武家屋敷街}} - Maruyama opened the private school {{ill|Shinsūsho|ja|神習処}} here in 1861. == External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/kensei/maruyamasakura National Diet Library Constitutional Archive Maruyama Sakura Documents (Microfilm: Private Collection)] {{S-start}} {{S-off}} {{Succession box | title = {{Flagicon|JPN}} {{ill|Lower Chamber Deputy Minister|ja|集議院下局次官}} | years = 1869
(Co-served with {{ill|Kanda Mokkaku|ja|神田孟恪}}) | before = {{ill|Kanda Takahira|ja|神田孝平}} | after = {{ill|Ano Tadamichi|ja|阿野公誠}} }} {{S-end}} {{Normdaten}} {{People-stub}} [[Category:19th-century He was also known by the names Masatora, Masamichi, and Sakura. His titles included Shinsūsho, Sokō, and Iwanoya. His wife's name was Umekichi (Okutsuki). == Career == Maruyama was born in Edo’s Shiba Mita district as the eldest son of Maruyama Masanao, a samurai of the {{ill|Shimabara Domain|ja|島原藩}}. During the late Edo period, he studied {{ill|Kokugaku|ja|国学}} of the {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}} school and became active in the royalist movement. In [[1869]] (Meiji 2), he became [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Daisuke]] and was dispatched to {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}} to engage in negotiations with Russia. Returning in [[1870]] (Meiji 3), he advocated an aggressive policy against Russian expansion, but his proposals were not adopted. He also supported {{ill|Sada Hakaru|ja|佐田白茅}}’s {{ill|Seikanron|ja|征韓論}} and urged its implementation, but this too was rejected. In November of the same year, he was reassigned to {{ill|Ken-Daisuke (Ministry of Foreign Affairs)|ja|外務権大丞}} due to the failure of the Karafuto negotiations. Maruyama, along with {{ill|Hatake Kiyotada|ja|畑経世}}, a samurai of the {{ill|Sasayama Domain|ja|篠山藩}}, attempted to carry out the rejected Seikanron through rebellion. However, in [[1871]] (Meiji 4), their plans were uncovered, and Maruyama was arrested on [[May 10]] while on duty at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. On [[May 29]], he was dismissed from his position as Ken-Daisuke. The incident, considered politically minor, led to his sentencing to life imprisonment. He was released in [[1880]] (Meiji 13) through amnesty. After his release, Maruyama opposed the {{ill|Freedom and People's Rights Movement|ja|自由民権運動}} by establishing the {{ill|Chūaisha|ja|忠愛社}} and publishing the newspaper {{ill|Meiji Nippo|ja|明治日報}}. In [[1882]] (Meiji 15), he co-founded the {{ill|Rikken Teiseitō|ja|立憲帝政党}} with {{ill|Fukuchi Gen'ichirō|ja|福地源一郎}} and worked as a conservative politician. In [[1890]] (Meiji 23), he was appointed {{ill|Gikanin|ja|元老院議官}} on [[June 12]] and became {{ill|Non-working official|ja|非職}} when the {{ill|Gikan|ja|元老院}} was abolished on [[October 20]]. He was subsequently named {{ill|Imperial appointee to the House of Peers|ja|貴族院勅選議員}} on [[September 29]], serving until his death on [[August 19]], [[1899]], due to valvular heart disease. Maruyama was also known as a {{ill|Manyōshū|ja|万葉集}}-style poet. == Honors == ; Rank * [[June 19]], [[1890]] (Meiji 23): [[Jūshi-i]]『官報』第2094号「叙任及辞令」1890年6月24日。 ; Medals * [[November 29]], [[1889]] (Meiji 22): {{ill|Constitution Promulgation Medal|ja|記念章#賞勲局所管の記念章}}『官報』第1935号「叙任及辞令」1889年12月9日。 == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Sources == * Shūgiin and Sangiin Editors, *{{ill|100-Year History of the Japanese Diet|ja|議会制度百年史}} - {{ill|House of Peers and Sangiin Member Directory|ja|貴族院・参議院議員名鑑}}*, 1990. * Maseo Watanabe and Junko Hirose, Editors, *{{ill|Records of Imperial Appointments|ja|国立公文書館所蔵 勅奏任官履歴原書}} Vol. 1*, Kashiwa Shobō, 1995. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Shimabara Samurai Residence Street|ja|島原武家屋敷街}} - Maruyama opened the private school {{ill|Shinsūsho|ja|神習処}} here in 1861. == External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/kensei/maruyamasakura National Diet Library Constitutional Archive Maruyama Sakura Documents (Microfilm: Private Collection)] {{S-start}} {{S-off}} {{Succession box | title = {{Flagicon|JPN}} {{ill|Lower Chamber Deputy Minister|ja|集議院下局次官}} | years = 1869
(Co-served with {{ill|Kanda Mokkaku|ja|神田孟恪}}) | before = {{ill|Kanda Takahira|ja|神田孝平}} | after = {{ill|Ano Tadamichi|ja|阿野公誠}} }} {{S-end}} {{Normdaten}} {{People-stub}} [[Category:19th-century {{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Maruyama Sakura]][[en:draft:Maruyama Sakura]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Maruyama Sakura|Maruyama Sakura]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Cite sources clearly|date=September 2015}} {{Politician | Native name = まるやま さくら | image = Maruyama_Sakura.jpg | image size = 200px | Country abbreviation = {{JPN}} | Birthdate = [[October 27]], [[1840]] | Birthplace = {{JPN}} Edo, Shiba Mita (present-day [[Minato, Tokyo|Minato, Tokyo]]) | Deathdate = {{Death date and age|1840|10|27|1899|8|19}} | Previous position = [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Ministry of Foreign Affairs]] [[Daisuke (official)|Daijō]] | Political party = [[Rikken Teiseitō]] | Titles and honors = [[Jūshi-i]]
[[Commemorative Medals#Promulgation of the Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Constitution Promulgation Medal]] | Office = [[House of Peers (Japan)|Member of the House of Peers]] | Start date = [[September 29]], [[1890]] | End date = [[August 19]], 1899 | Flag = JPN | Office2 = [[Gikanin|Gikan]] | Start date2 = [[June 12]], [[1890]] | End date2 = [[October 20]], 1890 | Flag2 = JPN }} '''Maruyama Sakura''' (丸山 作楽, {{ill|1840|ja|天保}} 11th year {{ill|October 3 (old calendar)|ja|10月3日 (旧暦)}} – [[August 19]], [[1899]]) was a [[Meiji period]] [[diplomat]], [[businessman]], and [[politician]]. He was also known by the names Masatora, Masamichi, and Sakura. His titles included Shinsūsho, Sokō, and Iwanoya. His wife's name was Umekichi (Okutsuki). == Career == Maruyama was born in Edo’s Shiba Mita district as the eldest son of Maruyama Masanao, a samurai of the {{ill|Shimabara Domain|ja|島原藩}}. During the late Edo period, he studied {{ill|Kokugaku|ja|国学}} of the {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}} school and became active in the royalist movement. In [[1869]] (Meiji 2), he became [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Daisuke]] and was dispatched to {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}} to engage in negotiations with Russia. Returning in [[1870]] (Meiji 3), he advocated an aggressive policy against Russian expansion, but his proposals were not adopted. He also supported {{ill|Sada Hakaru|ja|佐田白茅}}’s {{ill|Seikanron|ja|征韓論}} and urged its implementation, but this too was rejected. In November of the same year, he was reassigned to {{ill|Ken-Daisuke (Ministry of Foreign Affairs)|ja|外務権大丞}} due to the failure of the Karafuto negotiations. Maruyama, along with {{ill|Hatake Kiyotada|ja|畑経世}}, a samurai of the {{ill|Sasayama Domain|ja|篠山藩}}, attempted to carry out the rejected Seikanron through rebellion. However, in [[1871]] (Meiji 4), their plans were uncovered, and Maruyama was arrested on [[May 10]] while on duty at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. On [[May 29]], he was dismissed from his position as Ken-Daisuke. The incident, considered politically minor, led to his sentencing to life imprisonment. He was released in [[1880]] (Meiji 13) through amnesty. After his release, Maruyama opposed the {{ill|Freedom and People's Rights Movement|ja|自由民権運動}} by establishing the {{ill|Chūaisha|ja|忠愛社}} and publishing the newspaper {{ill|Meiji Nippo|ja|明治日報}}. In [[1882]] (Meiji 15), he co-founded the {{ill|Rikken Teiseitō|ja|立憲帝政党}} with {{ill|Fukuchi Gen'ichirō|ja|福地源一郎}} and worked as a conservative politician. In [[1890]] (Meiji 23), he was appointed {{ill|Gikanin|ja|元老院議官}} on [[June 12]] and became {{ill|Non-working official|ja|非職}} when the {{ill|Gikan|ja|元老院}} was abolished on [[October 20]]. He was subsequently named {{ill|Imperial appointee to the House of Peers|ja|貴族院勅選議員}} on [[September 29]], serving until his death on [[August 19]], [[1899]], due to valvular heart disease. Maruyama was also known as a {{ill|Manyōshū|ja|万葉集}}-style poet. == Honors == ; Rank * [[June 19]], [[1890]] (Meiji 23): [[Jūshi-i]]『官報』第2094号「叙任及辞令」1890年6月24日。 ; Medals * [[November 29]], [[1889]] (Meiji 22): {{ill|Constitution Promulgation Medal|ja|記念章#賞勲局所管の記念章}}『官報』第1935号「叙任及辞令」1889th year 12月9日。 == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Sources == * Shūgiin and Sangiin Editors, *{{ill|100-Year History of the Japanese Diet|ja|議会制度百年史}} - {{ill|House of Peers and Sangiin Member Directory|ja|貴族院・参議院議員名鑑}}*, 1990. * Maseo Watanabe and Junko Hirose, Editors, *{{ill|Records of Imperial Appointments|ja|国立公文書館所蔵 勅奏任官履歴原書}} Vol. 1*, Kashiwa Shobō, 1995. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Shimabara Samurai Residence Street|ja|島原武家屋敷街}} - Maruyama opened the private school {{ill|Shinsūsho|ja|神習処}} here in 1861. == External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/kensei/maruyamasakura National Diet Library Constitutional Archive Maruyama Sakura Documents (Microfilm: Private Collection)] {{S-start}} {{S-off}} {{Succession box | title = {{Flagicon|JPN}} {{ill|Lower Chamber Deputy Minister|ja|集議院下局次官}} | years = 1869
(Co-served with {{ill|Kanda Mokkaku|ja|神田孟恪}}) | before = {{ill|Kanda Takahira|ja|神田孝平}} | after = {{ill|Ano Tadamichi|ja|阿野公誠}} }} {{S-end}} {{Normdaten}} {{People-stub}} [[Category:19th-century Japanese businessmen]] [[Category:Members of the House of Peers (Japan)]] [[Category:Imperial appointees to the House of Peers]] [[Category:Meiji-era politicians]] [[Category:Japanese diplomats]] In [[1882]] (Meiji 15), he co-founded the {{ill|Rikken Teiseitō|ja|立憲帝政党}} with {{ill|Fukuchi Gen'ichirō|ja|福地源一郎}} and worked as a conservative politician. In [[1890]] (Meiji 23), he was appointed {{ill|Gikanin|ja|元老院議官}} on [[June 12]] and became {{ill|Non-working official|ja|非職}} when the {{ill|Gikan|ja|元老院}} was abolished on [[October 20]]. He was subsequently named {{ill|Imperial appointee to the House of Peers|ja|貴族院勅選議員}} on [[September 29]], serving until his death on [[August 19]], [[1899]], due to valvular heart disease. Maruyama was also known as a {{ill|Manyōshū|ja|万葉集}}-style poet. == Honors == ; Rank * [[June 19]], [[1890]] (Meiji 23): [[Jūshi-i]]『官報』第2094号「叙任及辞令」1890th year 6月24日。 ; Medals * [[November 29]], [[1889]] (Meiji 22): {{ill|Constitution Promulgation Medal|ja|記念章#賞勲局所管の記念章}}『官報』第1935号「叙任及辞令」1889th year 12月9日。 == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Sources == * Shūgiin and Sangiin Editors, *{{ill|100-Year History of the Japanese Diet|ja|議会制度百年史}} - {{ill|House of Peers and Sangiin Member Directory|ja|貴族院・参議院議員名鑑}}*, 1990. * Maseo Watanabe and Junko Hirose, Editors, *{{ill|Records of Imperial Appointments|ja|国立公文書館所蔵 勅奏任官履歴原書}} Vol. 1*, Kashiwa Shobō, 1995. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Shimabara Samurai Residence Street|ja|島原武家屋敷街}} - Maruyama opened the private school {{ill|Shinsūsho|ja|神習処}} here in 1861. == External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/kensei/maruyamasakura National Diet Library Constitutional Archive Maruyama Sakura Documents (Microfilm: Private Collection)] {{S-start}} {{S-off}} {{Succession box | title = {{Flagicon|JPN}} {{ill|Lower Chamber Deputy Minister|ja|集議院下局次官}} | years = 1869

[[Commemorative Medals#Promulgation of the Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Constitution Promulgation Medal]] | Office = [[House of Peers (Japan)|Member of the House of Peers]] | Start date = [[September 29]], [[1890]] | End date = [[August 19]], 1899 | Flag = JPN | Office2 = [[Gikanin|Gikan]] | Start date2 = [[June 12]], [[1890]] | End date2 = [[October 20]], 1890 | Flag2 = JPN }} '''Maruyama Sakura''' (丸山 作楽, {{ill|1840|ja|天保}} 11th year {{ill|October 3 (old calendar)|ja|10月3日 (旧暦)|1=October 3 (Lunisolar)}} – [[August 19]], [[1899]]) was a [[Meiji period]] [[diplomat]], [[businessman]], and [[politician]]. He was also known by the names Masatora, Masamichi, and Sakura. His titles included Shinsūsho, Sokō, and Iwanoya. His wife's name was Umekichi (Okutsuki). == Career == Maruyama was born in Edo’s Shiba Mita district as the eldest son of Maruyama Masanao, a samurai of the {{ill|Shimabara Domain|ja|島原藩}}. During the late Edo period, he studied {{ill|Kokugaku|ja|国学}} of the {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}} school and became active in the royalist movement. In [[1869]] (Meiji 2), he became [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Daisuke]] and was dispatched to {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}} to engage in negotiations with Russia. Returning in [[1870]] (Meiji 3), he advocated an aggressive policy against Russian expansion, but his proposals were not adopted. He also supported {{ill|Sada Hakaru|ja|佐田白茅}}’s {{ill|Seikanron|ja|征韓論}} and urged its implementation, but this too was rejected. In November of the same year, he was reassigned to {{ill|Ken-Daisuke (Ministry of Foreign Affairs)|ja|外務権大丞}} due to the failure of the Karafuto negotiations. Maruyama, along with {{ill|Hatake Kiyotada|ja|畑経世}}, a samurai of the {{ill|Sasayama Domain|ja|篠山藩}}, attempted to carry out the rejected Seikanron through rebellion. However, in [[1871]] (Meiji 4), their plans were uncovered, and Maruyama was arrested on [[May 10]] while on duty at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. On [[May 29]], he was dismissed from his position as Ken-Daisuke. The incident, considered politically minor, led to his sentencing to life imprisonment. He was released in [[1880]] (Meiji 13) through amnesty. After his release, Maruyama opposed the {{ill|Freedom and People's Rights Movement|ja|自由民権運動}} by establishing the {{ill|Chūaisha|ja|忠愛社}} and publishing the newspaper {{ill|Meiji Nippo|ja|明治日報}}. In [[1882]] (Meiji 15), he co-founded the {{ill|Rikken Teiseitō|ja|立憲帝政党}} with {{ill|Fukuchi Gen'ichirō|ja|福地源一郎}} and worked as a conservative politician. In [[1890]] (Meiji 23), he was appointed {{ill|Gikanin|ja|元老院議官}} on [[June 12]] and became {{ill|Non-working official|ja|非職}} when the {{ill|Gikan|ja|元老院}} was abolished on [[October 20]]. He was subsequently named {{ill|Imperial appointee to the House of Peers|ja|貴族院勅選議員}} on [[September 29]], serving until his death on [[August 19]], [[1899]], due to valvular heart disease. Maruyama was also known as a {{ill|Manyōshū|ja|万葉集}}-style poet. == Honors == ; Rank * [[June 19]], [[1890]] (Meiji 23): [[Jūshi-i]]『官報』第2094号「叙任及辞令」1890th year 6月24日。 ; Medals * [[November 29]], [[1889]] (Meiji 22): {{ill|Constitution Promulgation Medal|ja|記念章#賞勲局所管の記念章}}『官報』第1935号「叙任及辞令」1889th year 12月9日。 == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Sources == * Shūgiin and Sangiin Editors, *{{ill|100-Year History of the Japanese Diet|ja|議会制度百年史}} - {{ill|House of Peers and Sangiin Member Directory|ja|貴族院・参議院議員名鑑}}*, 1990. * Maseo Watanabe and Junko Hirose, Editors, *{{ill|Records of Imperial Appointments|ja|国立公文書館所蔵 勅奏任官履歴原書}} Vol. 1*, Kashiwa Shobō, 1995. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Shimabara Samurai Residence Street|ja|島原武家屋敷街}} - Maruyama opened the private school {{ill|Shinsūsho|ja|神習処}} here in 1861. == External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/kensei/maruyamasakura National Diet Library Constitutional Archive Maruyama Sakura Documents (Microfilm: Private Collection)] {{S-start}} {{S-off}} {{Succession box | title = {{Flagicon|JPN}} {{ill|Lower Chamber Deputy Minister|ja|集議院下局次官}} | years = 1869

It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Cite sources clearly|date=September 2015}} {{Politician | Native name = まるやま さくら | image = Maruyama_Sakura.jpg | image size = 200px | Country abbreviation = {{JPN}} | Birthdate = [[October 27]], [[1840]] | Birthplace = {{JPN}} Edo, Shiba Mita (present-day [[Minato, Tokyo|Minato, Tokyo]]) | Deathdate = {{Death date and age|1840|10|27|1899|8|19}} | Previous position = [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Ministry of Foreign Affairs]] [[Daisuke (official)|Daijō]] | Political party = [[Rikken Teiseitō]] | Titles and honors = [[Jūshi-i]]
[[Commemorative Medals#Promulgation of the Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Constitution Promulgation Medal]] | Office = [[House of Peers (Japan)|Member of the House of Peers]] | Start date = [[September 29]], [[1890]] | End date = [[August 19]], 1899 | Flag = JPN | Office2 = [[Gikanin|Gikan]] | Start date2 = [[June 12]], [[1890]] | End date2 = [[October 20]], 1890 | Flag2 = JPN }} '''Maruyama Sakura''' (丸山 作楽, {{ill|1840|ja|天保}} 11th year {{ill|October 3 (old calendar)|ja|10月3日 (旧暦)|1=October 3 (Lunisolar)}} – [[August 19]], [[1899]]) was a [[Meiji period]] [[diplomat]], [[businessman]], and [[politician]]. He was also known by the names Masatora, Masamichi, and Sakura. His titles included Shinsūsho, Sokō, and Iwanoya. His wife's name was Umekichi (Okutsuki). == Career == Maruyama was born in Edo’s Shiba Mita district as the eldest son of Maruyama Masanao, a samurai of the {{ill|Shimabara Domain|ja|島原藩}}. During the late Edo period, he studied {{ill|Kokugaku|ja|国学}} of the {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}} school and became active in the royalist movement. In [[1869]] (Meiji 2), he became [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Daisuke]] and was dispatched to {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}} to engage in negotiations with Russia. Returning in [[1870]] (Meiji 3), he advocated an aggressive policy against Russian expansion, but his proposals were not adopted. He also supported {{ill|Sada Hakaru|ja|佐田白茅}}’s {{ill|Seikanron|ja|征韓論}} and urged its implementation, but this too was rejected. In November of the same year, he was reassigned to {{ill|Ken-Daisuke (Ministry of Foreign Affairs)|ja|外務権大丞}} due to the failure of the Karafuto negotiations. Maruyama, along with {{ill|Hatake Kiyotada|ja|畑経世}}, a samurai of the {{ill|Sasayama Domain|ja|篠山藩}}, attempted to carry out the rejected Seikanron through rebellion. However, in [[1871]] (Meiji 4), their plans were uncovered, and Maruyama was arrested on [[May 10]] while on duty at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. On [[May 29]], he was dismissed from his position as Ken-Daisuke. The incident, considered politically minor, led to his sentencing to life imprisonment. He was released in [[1880]] (Meiji 13) through amnesty. After his release, Maruyama opposed the {{ill|Freedom and People's Rights Movement|ja|自由民権運動}} by establishing the {{ill|Chūaisha|ja|忠愛社}} and publishing the newspaper {{ill|Meiji Nippo|ja|明治日報}}. In [[1882]] (Meiji 15), he co-founded the {{ill|Rikken Teiseitō|ja|立憲帝政党}} with {{ill|Fukuchi Gen'ichirō|ja|福地源一郎}} and worked as a conservative politician. In [[1890]] (Meiji 23), he was appointed {{ill|Gikanin|ja|元老院議官}} on [[June 12]] and became {{ill|Non-working official|ja|非職}} when the {{ill|Gikan|ja|元老院}} was abolished on [[October 20]]. He was subsequently named {{ill|Imperial appointee to the House of Peers|ja|貴族院勅選議員}} on [[September 29]], serving until his death on [[August 19]], [[1899]], due to valvular heart disease. Maruyama was also known as a {{ill|Manyōshū|ja|万葉集}}-style poet. == Honors == ; Rank * [[June 19]], [[1890]] (Meiji 23): [[Jūshi-i]]『官報』第2094号「叙任及辞令」1890th year 6月24日。 ; Medals * [[November 29]], [[1889]] (Meiji 22): {{ill|Constitution Promulgation Medal|ja|記念章#賞勲局所管の記念章}}『官報』第1935号「叙任及辞令」1889th year 12月9日。 == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Sources == * Shūgiin and Sangiin Editors, *{{ill|100-Year History of the Japanese Diet|ja|議会制度百年史}} - {{ill|House of Peers and Sangiin Member Directory|ja|貴族院・参議院議員名鑑}}*, 1990. * Maseo Watanabe and Junko Hirose, Editors, *{{ill|Records of Imperial Appointments|ja|国立公文書館所蔵 勅奏任官履歴原書}} Vol. 1*, Kashiwa Shobō, 1995. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Shimabara Samurai Residence Street|ja|島原武家屋敷街}} - Maruyama opened the private school {{ill|Shinsūsho|ja|神習処}} here in 1861. == External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/kensei/maruyamasakura National Diet Library Constitutional Archive Maruyama Sakura Documents (Microfilm: Private Collection)] {{S-start}} {{S-off}} {{Succession box | title = {{Flagicon|JPN}} {{ill|Lower Chamber Deputy Minister|ja|集議院下局次官}} | years = 1869
(Co-served with {{ill|Kanda Mokkaku|ja|神田孟恪}}) | before = {{ill|Kanda Takahira|ja|神田孝平}} | after = {{ill|Ano Tadamichi|ja|阿野公誠}} }} {{S-end}} {{Normdaten}} {{People-stub}} [[Category:19th-century Japanese businessmen]] [[Category:Members of the House of Peers (Japan)]] [[Category:Imperial appointees to the House of Peers]] [[Category:Meiji-era politicians]] [[Category:Japanese diplomats]] Maruyama was also known as a {{ill|Manyōshū|ja|万葉集}}-style poet. == Honors == ; Rank * [[June 19]], [[1890]] (Meiji 23): [[Jūshi-i]]『官報』第2094号「叙任及辞令」1890th year 6月24日。 ; Medals * [[November 29]], [[1889]] (Meiji 22): {{ill|Constitution Promulgation Medal|ja|記念章#賞勲局所管の記念章}}『官報』第1935号「叙任及辞令」1889th year 12月9日。 == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Sources == * Shūgiin and Sangiin Editors, *{{ill|100-Year History of the Japanese Diet|ja|議会制度百年史}} - {{ill|House of Peers and Sangiin Member Directory|ja|貴族院・参議院議員名鑑}}*, 1990. * Maseo Watanabe and Junko Hirose, Editors, *{{ill|Records of Imperial Appointments|ja|国立公文書館所蔵 勅奏任官履歴原書}} Vol. 1*, Kashiwa Shobō, 1995. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Shimabara Samurai Residence Street|ja|島原武家屋敷街}} - Maruyama opened the private school {{ill|Shinsūsho|ja|神習処}} here in 1861. == External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/kensei/maruyamasakura National Diet Library Constitutional Archive Maruyama Sakura Documents (Microfilm: Private Collection)] {{S-start}} {{S-off}} {{Succession box | title = {{Flagicon|JPN}} {{ill|Lower Chamber Deputy Minister|ja|集議院下局次官}} | years = 1869

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Maruyama Sakura]][[en:draft:Maruyama Sakura]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Maruyama Sakura|Maruyama Sakura]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{Cite sources clearly|date=September 2015}} {{Politician | Native name = まるやま さくら | image = Maruyama_Sakura.jpg | image size = 200px | Country abbreviation = {{JPN}} | Birthdate = [[October 27]], [[1840]] | Birthplace = {{JPN}} Edo, Shiba Mita (present-day [[Minato, Tokyo|Minato, Tokyo]]) | Deathdate = {{Death date and age|1840|10|27|1899|8|19}} | Previous position = [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Ministry of Foreign Affairs]] [[Daisuke (official)|Daijō]] | Political party = [[Rikken Teiseitō]] | Titles and honors = [[Jūshi-i]]
[[Commemorative Medals#Promulgation of the Constitution of the Empire of Japan|Constitution Promulgation Medal]] | Office = [[House of Peers (Japan)|Member of the House of Peers]] | Start date = [[September 29]], [[1890]] | End date = [[August 19]], 1899 | Flag = JPN | Office2 = [[Gikanin|Gikan]] | Start date2 = [[June 12]], [[1890]] | End date2 = [[October 20]], 1890 | Flag2 = JPN }} '''Maruyama Sakura''' (丸山 作楽, {{ill|1840|ja|天保}} 11th year {{ill|October 3 (old calendar)|ja|10月3日 (旧暦)|1=October 3 (Lunisolar)}} – [[August 19]], [[1899]]) was a [[Meiji period]] [[diplomat]], [[businessman]], and [[politician]]. He was also known by the names Masatora, Masamichi, and Sakura. His titles included Shinsūsho, Sokō, and Iwanoya. His wife's name was Umekichi (Okutsuki). == Career == Maruyama was born in Edo’s Shiba Mita district as the eldest son of Maruyama Masanao, a samurai of the {{ill|Shimabara Domain|ja|島原藩}}. During the late Edo period, he studied {{ill|Kokugaku|ja|国学}} of the {{ill|Hirata Atsutane|ja|平田篤胤}} school and became active in the royalist movement. In [[1869]] (Meiji 2), he became [[Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Japan)|Daisuke]] and was dispatched to {{ill|Karafuto|ja|樺太}} to engage in negotiations with Russia. Returning in [[1870]] (Meiji 3), he advocated an aggressive policy against Russian expansion, but his proposals were not adopted. He also supported {{ill|Sada Hakaru|ja|佐田白茅}}’s {{ill|Seikanron|ja|征韓論}} and urged its implementation, but this too was rejected. In November of the same year, he was reassigned to {{ill|Ken-Daisuke (Ministry of Foreign Affairs)|ja|外務権大丞}} due to the failure of the Karafuto negotiations. Maruyama, along with {{ill|Hatake Kiyotada|ja|畑経世}}, a samurai of the {{ill|Sasayama Domain|ja|篠山藩}}, attempted to carry out the rejected Seikanron through rebellion. However, in [[1871]] (Meiji 4), their plans were uncovered, and Maruyama was arrested on [[May 10]] while on duty at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs. On [[May 29]], he was dismissed from his position as Ken-Daisuke. The incident, considered politically minor, led to his sentencing to life imprisonment. He was released in [[1880]] (Meiji 13) through amnesty. After his release, Maruyama opposed the {{ill|Freedom and People's Rights Movement|ja|自由民権運動}} by establishing the {{ill|Chūaisha|ja|忠愛社}} and publishing the newspaper {{ill|Meiji Nippo|ja|明治日報}}. In [[1882]] (Meiji 15), he co-founded the {{ill|Rikken Teiseitō|ja|立憲帝政党}} with {{ill|Fukuchi Gen'ichirō|ja|福地源一郎}} and worked as a conservative politician. In [[1890]] (Meiji 23), he was appointed {{ill|Gikanin|ja|元老院議官}} on [[June 12]] and became {{ill|Non-working official|ja|非職}} when the {{ill|Gikan|ja|元老院}} was abolished on [[October 20]]. He was subsequently named {{ill|Imperial appointee to the House of Peers|ja|貴族院勅選議員}} on [[September 29]], serving until his death on [[August 19]], [[1899]], due to valvular heart disease. Maruyama was also known as a {{ill|Manyōshū|ja|万葉集}}-style poet. == Honors == ; Rank * [[June 19]], [[1890]] (Meiji 23): [[Jūshi-i]]『官報』第2094号「叙任及辞令」1890th year 6月24日。 ; Medals * [[November 29]], [[1889]] (Meiji 22): {{ill|Constitution Promulgation Medal|ja|記念章#賞勲局所管の記念章}}『官報』第1935号「叙任及辞令」1889th year 12月9日。 == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Sources == * Shūgiin and Sangiin Editors, *{{ill|100-Year History of the Japanese Diet|ja|議会制度百年史}} - {{ill|House of Peers and Sangiin Member Directory|ja|貴族院・参議院議員名鑑}}*, 1990. * Maseo Watanabe and Junko Hirose, Editors, *{{ill|Records of Imperial Appointments|ja|国立公文書館所蔵 勅奏任官履歴原書}} Vol. 1*, Kashiwa Shobō, 1995. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Shimabara Samurai Residence Street|ja|島原武家屋敷街}} - Maruyama opened the private school {{ill|Shinsūsho|ja|神習処}} here in 1861. == External Links == * [https://ndlsearch.ndl.go.jp/rnavi/kensei/maruyamasakura National Diet Library Constitutional Archive Maruyama Sakura Documents (Microfilm: Private Collection)] {{S-start}} {{S-off}} {{Succession box | title = {{Flagicon|JPN}} {{ill|Lower Chamber Deputy Minister|ja|集議院下局次官}} | years = 1869
(Co-served with {{ill|Kanda Mokkaku|ja|神田孟恪}}) | before = {{ill|Kanda Takahira|ja|神田孝平}} | after = {{ill|Ano Tadamichi|ja|阿野公誠}} }} {{S-end}} {{Normdaten}} {{People-stub}} [[Category:19th-century
